solution:

also $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{s}=\frac{\mathrm{kq} \cdot \mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{r}^2} \cdot \mathrm{r}$
Therefore dimensionally

\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
(A) Coefficient of viscosity:
$\eta = \frac{F \cdot dr}{A \cdot dv} = \frac{[MLT^{-2}][L]}{[L^2][LT^{-1}]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$ → I
(B) Surface tension:
$S = \frac{F}{L} = \frac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L]} = [MT^{-2}] = [ML^0T^{-2}]$ → II
(C) Pressure:
$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$ → III
(D) Surface energy:
$E = S \times A = [MT^{-2}][L^2] = [ML^2T^{-2}]$ → IV
Hence, A-I, B-II, C-III, D-IV.

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{AT} \\ & \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{A} \\ & \mu_0=\mathrm{MLT}^{-2} \mathrm{~A}^{-2} \\ & \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{Q}^{\mathrm{x}} \mu_0^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{I}^{\mathrm{z}}=[\mathrm{AT}]^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2} \mathrm{~A}^{-2}\right]^{\mathrm{y}}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{z}} \\ & \mathrm{MLT}^{-1}=\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{L}^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y}} \mathrm{A}^{-2 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z+x}} \\ & \text { Now; } \mathrm{y}=1 \\ & \mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y}=-1 \\ & -2 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}=0 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{z}=1\end{aligned}$

(B) Torque $(\vec{\tau})=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$
$(\vec{\tau})=\mathrm{L} \times \mathrm{MLT}^{-2}=\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}(\mathrm{IV})$
(C) Coefficient of viscosity $\Rightarrow F=\eta A \frac{d V}{d t}$
$\eta \rightarrow \mathrm{Pa} \cdot \mathrm{sec}$

(D) Gravitational constant (G)
$\begin{aligned}

& {[\mathrm{G}]=\frac{\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{r}^2}{\mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{MLT}^{-2} \times \mathrm{L}^2}{\mathrm{M}^2}=\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
According to the principle of dimensional homogeneity, the dimensions of each  terms of a dimensional equation on both sides are the same.

So, dimension of b and V will be same,  b=V 

And dimension of aV2 and P will be same, aV2=P

From above two relations, we have ab2=P

∴b2a=1P=ML-1T-2-1

Thus, M-1L1T2  is the dimension of compressibility.

And the SI unit of B is By3=N C-1⇒B=N m3 C-1

subject: Physics
solution:
We need to find the unit of $\frac{\epsilon E}{t}$.
From Maxwell's equations, the displacement current density is given by $J_d = \epsilon \frac{\partial E}{\partial t}$.
The term $\frac{\epsilon E}{t}$ has the same dimensions as displacement current density $J_d$.
Current density $J$ is defined as current per unit area, $J = \frac{I}{A}$.
The SI unit of current $I$ is Ampere (A) and the SI unit of area $A$ is $m^2$.
Therefore, the unit of current density is $A/m^2$.
Alternatively, using dimensions:
$[\epsilon] = M^{-1}L^{-3}T^4A^2$
$[E] = MLT^{-3}A^{-1}$
$[t] = T$
Dimension of $\frac{\epsilon E}{t} = \frac{(M^{-1}L^{-3}T^4A^2)(MLT^{-3}A^{-1})}{T} = L^{-2}A$.
This corresponds to the unit $A/m^2$.

subject: Physics
solution:
(A) Surface tension is related to force and length as  S=Fl=MLT-2 L=M T-2

=kg s-2 (IV)

(B) Pressure is related to force and area as P=FA

 =MLT-2 L2=kg m-1 s-2  (III)

(C) Viscosity in terms of force, area and velocity gradient is given by  η=FAdvdx=MLT-2 L2LT-1L

=ML-1 T-1=kg m-1 s-1 (I)

(D) Impulse is related to force and time of application as J=∫F dt=MLT-2×T

=MLT-1=kg m s-1 (II).

So A-(IV), B-(III), C-(I), D-(II)

(C) Electric field is stated as E=ForceCharge=MLT-2AT=M1L1T-3A-1   (IV)

(D) Latent heat is stated as L=HeatMass=Energy Mass=ML2T-2M=M0L2T-2     (I)
